title: Techniques for the detection and counting of starch particles in the atmosphere
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"ad3ei","text":"Suspended particles in the atmosphere (aerosols) can impact visibility, the radiation budget, architectural heritage, agricultural activity, and human health (Pérez et al., 2020). Recent studies suggest that flour derivatives like starch are present in the environment (de Diego et al., under revision). This research protocol outlines a list of suitable techniques for detecting and recording the presence of starch in the air. steps: Decide what type of sampling to deploy in the field ( fig.1 ). Figure 1 . Types of sampling methodologies, supporting surfaces, and counting techniques for airborne starch sampling
Prepare sticky slides spreading the adhesive mixture with a soft brush. The duration of exposure varies according to the research goals.

Several recipes are in the literature (eg. Käpylä, 1989). But we recommend the following one. 20 g, Silicone grease 500 mL, Acetone 500 mL, Carbon tetrachloride (optional) 60 rpm, Room temperature, 2 days, or intermittent manual procedure Weigh 20 g of silicone grease and add to a 1L caramel-colored jar. Pour the carbon tetrachloride into the jar and gently shake the bottle by opening the lid from time to time to evacuate any vapors that accumulate inside. Let it rest for a day and repeat the shaking operation. Generally, between 24 and 48 hours of this procedure are required until the silicone is completely dissolved. Once the silicone grease has completely dissolved, fill up with 500 mL of acetone. Carbon tetrachloride can be replaced with 500 mL of acetone, although the silicone solubility is lower and the procedure will require more time. This adhesive mixture can be prepared and stored in a cool place. It is useful for studying aerosols using gravimetric or volumetric methods. Depending on the ambient temperature, the solution may appear cloudy. This does not affect its use.

This sampling method is based on the volumetric-isokinetic technique designed by Hirst (1952) which is regularly used in Aerobiology. 7 day recording volumetric spore trap (Pollen and spore trap, Burkard, 1, https://burkard.co.uk/ , https://s3.amazonaws.com/protocols-files/files/q5ijcf9ff.jpg ) The trap (Burkard Manufacturing Co.) filters air at a constant rate of 0.6 m 3 per hour. The aerosols are collected on a sticky Melinex plastic tape (provided by the trap manufacturer) that rotates at a steady speed of 2 mm per hour, allowing for the calculation of hourly and daily concentrations (number of particles per m 3 ). The device faces the wind automatically. The tape is replaced weekly ( Fig. 2 ). Find additional details on the operating process in Burkard's manual. Figure 2 . Changing the sticky tape of the Burkard pollen and spore trap
After placing the Melinex tape on the rotating drum of the Burkard trap (please refer to the trap's user manual for instructions), it should be coated with the adhesive mixture using a soft brush.
The preferred mounting media is glycerine with a small amount of phenol to prevent fungal growth. Store in a bottle. 50 mL, Glycerol 2 mg, Phenol

Add 5 to 6 drops of mounting media (glycerol + phenol) to each exposed slide, cover them with a clean coverslip, and seal them with nail polish. To increase the fluidity of the mounting medium, it can be gently heated no more than 35 or 40°C in a water bath.

Samples are studied with an optical microscope with a final magnification of 400 or 1000X according to particle size. Binocular microscope (DM750, Leica, 1, https://www.leicabiosystems.com/es/ , https://s3.amazonaws.com/protocols-files/files/q5kzcf9ff.png ) Gravimetric samples require inspection of the total surface of the slide. By performing a simple calculation, we can determine the deposition rates in terms of the number of particles per square centimeter over a specific period. The reference area for this calculation is the area of the wooden slot. We performed one-month exposure periods. Starch granules appear under the optical microscope as hyaline particles that are difficult to separate from mineral dust like quartz crystals, which are very common in aerosol samples ( Fig. 4 a,c,e ) However, polarized light illumination allows starch to be recognized from a cross-shaped mark ( Fig. 4 b,d,f ) that identifies it among the rest of the aerosols in the sample (Moss, 1976). Figure 4 . Starch granules at 400 x magnification. Reference flour starch without (a) and with (b) polarized light. Starch granules from gravimetric samples without (c) and with (d) polarized light. Starch granules from volumetric samples without (e) and with (f) polarized light. Red arrows show starch granules.

The exposed tape should be cut into 7 pieces, each corresponding to a sampled day, using the Perspex cutting block provided by the trap manufacturer ( Fig. 3, 4, 5 ). All the materials and tools to be used should be carefully cleaned with ethanol. Figure 3 . Exposed Melinex tapes Figure 4 . Materials for mounting the slides Figure 5. Cutting the 7-day exposed tape

Samples are studied with an optical microscope with a final magnification of 400 or 1000X according to particle size. Binocular microscope (DM750, Leica, 1, https://www.leicabiosystems.com/es/ , https://s3.amazonaws.com/protocols-files/files/q5kzcf9ff.png ) Volumetric samples can be analyzed at daily or hourly intervals using the standard technique outlined in Käpylä and Penttinen (1981) for aerobiological samples. Starch granules appear under the optical microscope as hyaline particles that are difficult to separate from mineral dust like quartz crystals, which are very common in aerosol samples ( Fig. 9 a,c,e ) However, polarized light illumination allows starch to be recognized from a cross-shaped mark ( Fig. 9 b,d,f ) that identifies it among the rest of the aerosols in the sample (Moss, 1976). Figure 9 . Starch granules at 400 x magnification. Reference flour starch without (a) and with (b) polarized light. Starch granules from gravimetric samples without (c) and with (d) polarized light. Starch granules from volumetric samples without (e) and with (f) polarized light. Red arrows show starch granules.

This technique relies on gravity to deposit aerosols suspended in the air. We developed a wooden box trap that prevents the repulsion or attraction of aerosols caused by static charges ( Fig. 2, 3 ). The collected material is supported on a coated slide, and the particles enter through a known size slot. The box trap is positioned horizontally without any surrounding obstacles. Figure 2 . Box trap scheme Figure 3 . Wooden box trap. (a) Exterior and (b) interior view with the slide

Each piece of tape should be glued to a clean slide with the Gelvatol solution ( Fig. 6 ). Add the piece of tape to each slide ( Fig. 7 ). Figure 6 . Coating the slide with Gelvatol solution Figure 7. Fixing the 1-day piece of tape Take extreme care not to rotate the daily tape pieces so as not to lose the time sequence of the record.

Add 5 to 6 drops of mounting media (glycerol + phenol) to each slide, cover them with a clean coverslip, and seal them with nail polish ( Fig. 8 ). To increase the fluidity of the mounting medium, it can be gently heated no more than 35 or 40°C in a water bath. Figure 8 . Putting the coverslip

Preparing the Gelvatol solution 35 g, Polyvinyl alcohol (Gelvatol) 2 g, Phenol 80 mL, Distilled water Overnight Add the Gelvatol and the phenol to the water and allow it to stand overnight. 50 mL, Glycerol 100 Undetermined, 40 °C, The next day, add the glycerol, heat in a water bath (or warm gradually in a microwave), and stir to mix. Use this to prepare the 10% solution. Store tightly covered. It will last a long time.

10% Gelvatol solution 2 mL, Gelvatol solution (previous step) 18 mL, Distilled water Undetermined, 40 °C, Add the 2 ml of Gelvatol solution to the distilled water. Shake, stir, or gently heat in a water bath and mix until the Gelvatol completely disperses. Use this 10% Gelvatol solution to attach the 24-hour segments of exposed Melinex tape onto microscope slides. The Gelvatol solution is thick, so it is easier to weigh 2 g instead of measuring 2 ml solution.
creator: Claudio F. Pérez
